Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The board changed after pressing RIGHT. Let me compare the before and after frames to see what moved.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after frames to see what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_seg = before.segmentation
after_seg = after.segmentation

# Get positions of key objects in both frames
def get_obj_positions(seg):
    result = {}
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['pixels'] < 200:
            b = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in b]
            cols = [p[1] for p in b]
            result[n['hash'][:8]] = (sum(rows)//len(rows), sum(cols)//len(cols), n['color'])
    return result

before_pos = get_obj_positions(before_seg)
after_pos = get_obj_positions(after_seg)

print("Before positions:")
for k, v in sorted(before_pos.items()):
    print(f"  {k}: {v}")
print("\nAfter positions:")
for k, v in sorted(after_pos.items()):
    print(f"  {k}: {v}")

print("\nChanges:")
for k in set(list(before_pos.keys()) + list(after_pos.keys())):
    if before_pos.get(k) != after_pos.get(k):
        print(f"  {k}: {before_pos.get(k)} -> {after_pos.get(k)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The white dot (W, hash a57e9c94) moved from (31, 19) to (63, 63). The bottom bar also changed hash but stayed at (63, 31). The green squares and other objects stayed in place.
Wait, the white dot moved to (63, 63) which is the bottom-right corner - it seems like it disappeared or moved off-screen. This suggests that pressing RIGHT moved something, but not what I expected.
Let me look more carefully. The white dot (W) was inside the left green square (N). After pressing RIGHT, the white dot moved to (63, 63) - basically off the board. This might mean the green square with the white dot was "activated" or "moved" in some way.
Wait, let me reconsider. Looking at the ASCII again:
- The left green square (N) at rows 30-32, cols 18-20 has a white center (W) at (31, 19)
- The right green square (N) at rows 30-32, cols 27-29 has a black center (B) at (31, 28)
After pressing RIGHT, the white dot moved to (63, 63). This is strange. Let me check the current frame more carefully.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the current frame more carefully
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Current state around the green squares:")
for i in range(28, 35):
    print(f"{i:2d}: {lines[i][15:35]}")

# Check if the left green square changed
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N' or n['color'] == 'W' or n['color'] == 'B':
        print(f"\nid={n['id']} color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} center approx: {n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see what happened. The green squares moved! The left green square (N) with the white dot moved from cols 18-20 to cols 21-23 (moved right by 3). The green square with the black dot stayed at cols 27-29. The white dot that was at (31, 19) is now at (31, 22) - it moved with the green square. The W at (63, 63) is a leftover or something else.
Wait, let me re-check. Looking at the current ASCII:
- Row 30: `wwwwwwNNNwwwNNNwwwpp` - two green squares
- Row 31: `wwwwwwNWNwwwNBNwwwpp` - first has W center, second has B center
The left green square is now at cols 21-23 (center 22) and the right one is at cols 27-29 (center 28). Before the action, the left green square was at cols 18-20 (center 19).
So pressing RIGHT moved the left green square (with white dot) to the right by 3 cells. The white dot at (63, 63) is probably a leftover artifact.
Let me undo this and understand the mechanics better. Actually, let me first try LEFT to see if it moves back.
